name: Essential Farsi
description: The 1,000 most common Farsi words in frequency order, so every session is spent on words you will actually meet. Iranian Persian: script plus pronunciation on the front, meaning on the reveal, and the notes carry what textbooks bury: present stems for every verb, the spoken-Tehrani forms (mikhaham becomes mikham), and a real example phrase. A short primer on the sounds, the script, and the grammar glue (ezafe, ra, mi-) comes first so the words have somewhere to live. Keep new cards to 10-20 a day and say every answer out loud.

fields.front: Farsi, Persian, Dari: what is the language of this deck?
fields.back: Persian as spoken in Iran, called Farsi by its speakers. Dari (Afghanistan) and Tajik (Tajikistan) are close siblings; this vocabulary carries you far in all three.
fields.extra: Persian is an Indo-European language: grammar feels closer to French or English than to Arabic, even though it borrows the Arabic script and many Arabic words.

fields.front: Which direction does Persian writing run, and what do the letters do?
fields.back: Right to left, and letters join like cursive: most letters change shape depending on their position in the word.
fields.extra: Numbers are written left to right inside the right-to-left text. The alphabet has 32 letters: Arabic's 28 plus pe, che, zhe, gaf for p, ch, zh, g.

fields.front: کتاب is spelled k-t-a-b but pronounced ketab. What is missing?
fields.back: The short vowels a, e, o are simply not written. Only the long vowels a (aa), i, u get letters.
fields.extra: This is why every card in this deck shows the pronunciation in Latin letters: you cannot reliably guess it from the spelling. You learn each word's sound with its shape.

fields.front: The six Persian vowels, as romanized in this deck: a, e, o, ā, i, u. How do they sound?
fields.back: a as in cat, e as in bed, o as in go (short), ā deep like the a in father or saw, i as in machine, u as in rule.
fields.extra: The macron matters: bad (bad) and bād (wind) are different words. ā is the most Persian-sounding vowel; open the back of your mouth.

fields.front: The sound written kh in this deck (the letter خ): how do you say it?
fields.back: A raspy h from the back of the throat, like the ch in Bach or Scottish loch.
fields.extra: Common everywhere: khub (good), khāne (house), khodā (God). It is a single sound, not k plus h.

fields.front: The sound written gh in this deck (the letters ق and غ): how do you say it?
fields.back: A gargled g deep in the throat, close to a French r. In Iran the two letters sound identical.
fields.extra: Examples: ghazā (food), āghā (sir), ghahve (coffee). Do not replace it with a plain g; the depth is what makes it Persian.

fields.front: The apostrophe in words like ba'd (after) and sā'at (hour): what sound is it?
fields.back: A glottal stop, the catch in the middle of English uh-oh. It comes from the letters ع and ء.
fields.extra: In relaxed speech it often just lengthens the vowel: ba'd sounds like baad. Do not stress about producing it perfectly.

fields.front: Persian has four letters for z and three for s. How many sounds is that?
fields.back: One z and one s. Arabic loanwords keep their original spelling, but Persian pronounces س ص ث all as s, and ز ذ ض ظ all as z.
fields.extra: Likewise ت ط are both t and ه ح are both h. Reading is easy; spelling is the hard part, like English.

fields.front: ketāb-e man = my book; dar-e bozorg = the big door. What is the little -e doing?
fields.back: That is ezāfe: an unstressed -e that links a noun to its owner or its adjective. Noun -e description, noun -e owner.
fields.extra: It is usually not written in the script at all; you just say it. After vowels it becomes -ye: khāne-ye man = my house. Master ezāfe and half of Persian phrasing unlocks.

fields.front: Man fārsi yād migiram = I am learning Farsi, literally: I Farsi learn. Where does the Persian verb go?
fields.back: At the end. Persian is subject-object-verb.
fields.extra: The verb ending already shows the person (migiram = I take), so subject pronouns are often dropped: fārsi yād migiram is a complete sentence.

fields.front: How many grammatical genders and articles does Persian have?
fields.back: None. No gender (u means both he and she), no el/la, no der/die/das, and adjectives never agree with anything.
fields.extra: Persian grammar is famously learner-friendly: the hard parts are the script and the sounds, not the endings.

fields.front: Man ketāb rā khāndam = I read the book. What is rā?
fields.back: The definite direct-object marker: it follows the thing the verb acts on, when that thing is specific.
fields.extra: In speech rā becomes ro (after vowels) or o: ketābo khundam. You will hear ro and o constantly; they are the same word.

fields.front: raftan = to go, stem rav. What does mi-rav-am mean, piece by piece?
fields.back: mi- (ongoing) + rav (go) + -am (I) = I go, I am going. Present tense is mi + present stem + person ending.
fields.extra: Endings: -am I, -i you, -ad he/she (spoken -e), -im we, -id you pl., -and they (spoken -an). Every verb card in this deck gives you the stem.

fields.front: Written: mikhāham beravam. Spoken in Tehran: mikhām beram. What happened?
fields.back: Everyday Iranian speech compresses words: ān to un, khāne to khune, mikhāham to mikhām, ast to e. Both registers are the same language.
fields.extra: This deck teaches the written form as the headword and gives the spoken form in the notes. Learn both; read the first, say the second.

fields.front: How do you make a Persian noun plural?
fields.back: Add -hā: ketāb, ketābhā = book, books. In speech it shortens to -ā: ketābā.
fields.extra: Animate nouns can also take -ān (mardān, men), and Arabic loans sometimes keep Arabic plurals, but -hā always works and is what you will say.

fields.front: ketāb, zendegi, irāni: which syllable gets the stress?
fields.back: The last one: keTĀB, zendeGI, irāNI. Persian words are stressed on the final syllable by default.
fields.extra: Main exception: verb prefixes steal the stress, MI-ram, BE-ro, NA-ro. Get final stress right and you instantly sound less foreign.

fields.front: An Iranian taxi driver refuses your money: ghābel nadāre, it is not worthy of you. Do you keep your money?
fields.back: No. This is ta'ārof, ritual politeness: offers and refusals exchanged for courtesy. Insist two or three times and then pay.
fields.extra: Ta'ārof runs through the whole language: befarmāid (please, go ahead), khāhesh mikonam (you are welcome), dast-e shomā dard nakone (thank you, literally: may your hand not hurt).
Literally: peace. Works any time of day, formal or not. Reply: salām!
Literally: may God protect you. Spoken: khodāfez. Casual alternative: felan (see you, for now).
The safe yes with strangers and elders. Casual yes: āre. Never answer an elder with āre if you can help it.
The everyday yes among friends and family. Polite version: bale.
Doubling it softens it: na na = no no, do not worry. Nakheyr is the formal no.
Kheyli mamnun = thanks a lot. Also common: mersi (yes, from French), and the formal motashakkeram.
Attach to any request: ye chāy, lotfan = one tea, please. Softer and very Persian: khāhesh mikonam.
The standard reply to mamnun. Literally: I request. Also used to press an offer politely.
Gets attention, apologizes, or opens a question to a stranger: bebakhshid, ... = excuse me, ...
Man irāni hastam = I am Iranian. With rā: man rā (spoken: mano) = me.
For friends, family, kids. To kojāyi? = where are you? Use shomā with strangers.
One pronoun for both genders. Spoken Tehrani says un instead: un kojāst? = where is he/she?
Mā irāni hastim = we are Iranian.
The safe you for anyone you just met, like French vous. Shomā khubid? = are you well?
Spoken: unā. Ānhā injā hastand = they are here.
In chiye? = what is this? (spoken). In ketāb = this book.
Spoken: un. Ān mard = that man (spoken: un mard).
Spoken it shrinks to -o: man o to = you and me. Chāy va ghahve = tea and coffee.
Goftam ke miām = I said that I am coming. Also glues descriptions: mardi ke didi = the man that you saw.
Az irān = from Iran. Also comparisons: behtar az man = better than me.
Be tehrān miram = I am going to Tehran. Spoken it often glues on: behesh goftam = I told (to) him.
Formal in writing: dar irān = in Iran. Speech prefers tu: tu khune = in the house. Same spelling also means door.
Bā man biā = come with me. Bā ham = together.
Barāye to = for you. Spoken: barā-ye or just vāse: vāse chi? = what for?

fields.back: until; so that; as many as
fields.extra: Tā fardā = until tomorrow. Also counts: se tā sib = three (units of) apple; spoken Persian counts everything with tā.
Follows a specific object: ketāb rā bede = give the book. Spoken: ro or o (ketābo bede). See the primer card.
Man ham = me too. Doubles up: ham in, ham ān = both this and that.
Chāy yā ghahve? = tea or coffee? Yā ... yā ... = either ... or ...
Written and spoken both. Its twin vali is more casual; they are interchangeable.
The but you will actually hear: khubam, vali khaste-am = I am fine, but tired.
Spoken: age. Age biāi = if you come. Pairs with mi- or the be- subjunctive, not with will.
Nayāmadam chon khaste budam = I did not come because I was tired. Also: chonke.
Pas boro = then go. Also: pas chi? = well then what?, of course.
The conversation oiler: khob, berim? = well, shall we go? Not the same as khub (good), though related.
Faghat yeki = just one. Faghat man midunam = only I know.
The all-purpose intensifier: kheyli khub = very good. Kheyli mamnun = thanks a lot.
Hame midunand = everyone knows. Hame chiz = everything. Hame jā = everywhere.
Har ruz = every day. Har kas = whoever. Har chi = whatever (spoken).
Needs a negative verb: hich kas nayāmad = nobody came. Alone as an answer: hich = nothing, never mind.
Spoken: ye. Ye chāy lotfan = a tea please. Also the number 1.
Do tā chāy = two teas. Dotā = a pair, two of them (spoken).
Se ruz = three days. Sad o se = 103.
Present is the endings on words: khubam = I am fine, khubi = you are fine, khube = it is fine. Past: budam, budi, bud = I was, you were, he was.
Hast emphasizes existence: chāy hast? = is there tea? Ali injāst = Ali is here (ast glued as -st).
Moshkeli nist = no problem, it is nothing. Ali injā nist = Ali is not here.
The written is: in khub ast. In speech it is just -e: in khube. You will read ast constantly and almost never say it.
Stem: dār. Dāram = I have. Pul nadāram = I have no money. Also builds the progressive: dāram miram = I am (in the middle of) going.
Stem: kon. Mikonam = I do. Chikār mikoni? = what are you doing? The engine of hundreds of compound verbs: fekr kardan, komak kardan ...
Stem: shav (spoken: sh). Chi shod? = what happened? Mishe = it becomes / it is possible: mishe beram? = may I go?
Stem: khāh. Mikhāham (spoken: mikhām) = I want. Chi mikhāy? = what do you want? Followed by the be- form: mikhām beram = I want to go.
Stem: tavān (spoken: tun). Mitavānam, spoken mitunam = I can. Nemitunam biām = I cannot come.
Stem: rav (spoken: r). Miravam, spoken miram = I am going. Kojā miri? = where are you going? Boro! = go!
Stem: ā. Miāyam, spoken miām = I am coming. Biā! = come! Umad = he came (spoken).
Stem: gu (spoken: g). Miguyam, spoken migam = I say. Chi gofti? = what did you say? Begu = say it.
Stem: bin. Mibinam = I see. Mibinamet = see you (I see you). Didi? = did you see?
Stem: dān (spoken: dun). Midānam, spoken midunam = I know. Nemidunam = I do not know, the most useful sentence in any language.
Stem: deh (spoken: d). Midaham, spoken midam = I give. Bede = give it. Behem bede = give it to me.

fields.back: to take, to get, to catch
fields.extra: Stem: gir. Migiram = I take. Begir = take it. Also: yād gereftan = to learn, taksi gereftan = to catch a taxi.
Never changes form; the next verb carries the person: bāyad beram = I must go, bāyad bere = he must go.
Shāyad biām = maybe I will come. A frozen form of shāyestan, to be fitting: it may well be.
Khubi? = are you okay? (the everyday how-are-you). Khube = it is fine, okay.
Yes, it sounds like English bad and means bad. Bad nist = not bad, the national verdict on most things.
Khāne-ye bozorg = a big house. Mādar-bozorg = grandmother, pedar-bozorg = grandfather.
Spoken: kuchik. Ye chāy-e kuchik = a small tea. Kuchulu = tiny, cute-small (for kids).
Ye chiz begam? = can I say something? Hame chiz = everything. Chizi nist = it is nothing.
Chikār mikoni? = what are you doing? Kār dāram = I am busy (I have things to do). Sar-e kār = at work.
Vaght nadāram = I have no time. Vaghti = when (as a connector): vaghti umad = when he came. Hich vaght = never.
Ruz-e khub = a good day. Emruz = today, har ruz = every day. Ruz-etun bekheyr = good day (polite).
Shab bekheyr = good night. Emshab = tonight. Dishab = last night.
Emsāl = this year, pārsāl = last year. Sāl-e now mobārak = happy new year (Nowruz greeting).
Spoken: khune. Miram khune = I am going home. Khāne-ye man = my house.
Rāh-āhan = railway (iron road). Tu rāham = I am on my way. Rāh raftan = to walk.
Injā = here (this place), unjā = there, hame jā = everywhere. Jā hast? = is there room?
Harf zadan = to talk. Harfāsh = his words, what he says. Harf-e man ine ke ... = my point is that ...
Esmet chiye? = what is your name? (spoken). Esm-e man ... = my name is ...
Pul nadāram = I have no money. Pul-dār = rich (money-having). Cash culture: always carry some.
Ye livān āb = a glass of water. Āb-e ma'dani = mineral water. Āberu (water of the face) = honor, face.
Dast-e shomā dard nakone = thank you (may your hand not hurt), said for food, favors, work. Dast bede = shake hands.
Sar-e kār = at work. Sardard = headache. Sar-e sā'at = on time. Endlessly productive in idioms.
Delam mikhād = I feel like (having) it. Delam barāt tang shode = I miss you (my heart has tightened for you). The anatomical heart is ghalb.
Also the magic word of service: cheshm = yes, consider it done (on my eye). Cheshmāt = your eyes.
Plural: mardhā or mardān. Mard-e khub = a good man.
Zan-e man = my wife. Zanhā = women. Zan o shohar = wife and husband, a couple.
Bachchehā = kids, also hey guys among friends. Bachche dāri? = do you have kids?
Pesar-e man = my son. Pesar-khāle = cousin (aunt's son), also: overly familiar guy.
Dokhtar-e man = my daughter. Dokhtar-amu = cousin (uncle's daughter).
Spoken affectionately: bābā. Pedar-bozorg = grandfather. Pedar o mādar = parents.
Spoken affectionately: māmān. Mādar-bozorg = grandmother. Note how close to English mother; same ancient root.
Dust-e man = my friend. Crucially: dust dāshtan = to like, to love. Dustet dāram = I love you.
Everywhere in daily speech: khodā rā shokr = thank God, be omid-e khodā = God willing, ey khodā = oh God.
Āghā-ye Ahmadi = Mr. Ahmadi. Also flags down a waiter: āghā! Bebakhshid āghā = excuse me sir.
Khānom-e Ahmadi = Ms. Ahmadi. Polite address for any woman. Khānomhā va āghāyān = ladies and gentlemen.
Hālā chi? = now what? Hālā hālāhā = not for a long while yet. Softer than alān.
Alān miām = I am coming right now (the famous Iranian right now: could be five minutes, could be an hour).
Em- marks nearness: emruz today, emshab tonight, emsāl this year. Emruz chandome? = what date is it today?
Fardā mibinamet = see you tomorrow. Pas-fardā = the day after tomorrow.
Diruz umad = he came yesterday. Pariruz = the day before yesterday.
In (this) + jā (place). Biā injā = come here. Injās = he/it is here (spoken).
Spoken: unjā. Unjā bud = he was there. Literally: that place.
Kojāyi? = where are you? Kojā miri? = where are you going? Dastshuyi kojāst? = where is the bathroom?
Ki bud? = who was it? In kiye? = who is this? (spoken). Same spelling as key (when); context decides.
Key miāy? = when are you coming? Az key? = since when? Same spelling as ki (who).
Che khabar? = what news, what's up? Che khub! = how nice! In questions before nouns: che rangi? = what color?
The what you will actually hear: chi shod? = what happened? Chi gofti? = what did you say? Written form: che.
Cherā na? = why not? Also the yes to a negative question: nemiāy? cherā! = are you not coming? Yes I am!
Chetori? = how are you? (spoken). Chetor mishe ...? = how does one ...?

fields.back: how many; how much; a few
fields.extra: Chand-e? = how much is it? (the market question). Chand tā? = how many? Chand ruz = a few days.
Che + ghadr (amount). Cheghadr shod? = how much (do I owe)? Cheghadr khoshgele! = how pretty it is!
Spoken: kodum. Kodum yeki? = which one? Kodum behtar-e? = which is better?
Az sefr shoru' kardam = I started from zero. English cipher and zero descend from the same Arabic root, sifr.
Spoken: chār. Chahārshanbe = Wednesday (fourth day). Chahār rāh = intersection (four ways).
Panj daghighe = five minutes. Panjshanbe = Thursday. Related to English punch (the five-ingredient drink).
Spoken: shish. Shish tā = six of them. Sā'at-e shish = six o'clock.
Hafte (week) is its child: seven days. Haft-sin: the seven S items on the Nowruz table.
Sā'at-e hasht-e sobh = eight in the morning. Hashtād = eighty.
Same spelling as na (no); the vowel differs: noh. Sā'at-e noh = nine o'clock.
Dah tā = ten of them. Dah hezār toman = ten thousand toman. The same spelling read deh means village.
The teens are yak/do/se + dah fused: yāzdah, davāzdah, sizdah ... Sā'at-e yāzdah = eleven o'clock.
Davāzdah-e zohr = twelve noon. Sizdah = 13, chahārdah = 14, pānzdah = 15, and so on to nuzdah = 19.
Bist o yek = 21 (twenty and one); all compounds use o (and). Also: a perfect score at school is bist.
Si ruz = thirty days. Si o se pol: the famous 33-arch bridge in Esfahan.
Chehel sāl = forty years. Chehel Sotun: the Forty Columns palace in Esfahan.
Panjāh darsad = fifty percent. The other tens: shast 60, haftād 70, hashtād 80, navad 90.
Shast sāl = sixty years. Note the spelling with sād; the thumb (shast) is spelled شست.
Haftād o panj = 75. Dahe-ye haftād = the (Iranian) seventies.
Hashtād sāl = eighty years. Hasht (8) + -ād, the tens suffix.
Navad daghighe = ninety minutes, a football match. Navad o noh = 99.
Sad sāl be in sālhā = may you see a hundred such years (birthday wish). Sad dar sad = one hundred percent.
Dah hezār = ten thousand. Hezār bār goftam = I told you a thousand times.
Ye milyun toman = a million toman. Milyuner = millionaire.
Avvalin bār = the first time. Avval-e sāl = the start of the year. Az avval = from the beginning.
Dovvom, sevvom, chahārom: ordinals add -om. Dast-e dovvom = second-hand.
Ākharin bār = the last time. Ākhar-e hafte = the weekend. Ākharesh chi shod? = how did it end?
Nesf-e shab = midnight (half of night). Nesfesh māl-e to = half of it is yours.
Sā'at chand-e? = what time is it? Sā'at-e se = three o'clock. Ye sā'at = one hour.
Ye daghighe sabr kon = wait a minute. Panj daghighe dige = in five more minutes.
Ye sāniye = one second, hold on. Chand sāniye = a few seconds.
Ye lahze = one moment, please. Hamin lahze = this very moment.
In hafte = this week. Hafte-ye āyande = next week. Ākhar-e hafte = weekend. From haft, seven.
Māh-e āyande = next month. Also the moon, and a compliment: māh-i = you are lovely (moon-like).
Sobh bekheyr = good morning. Emruz sobh = this morning. Sobh-e zud = early morning.
Ba'd az zohr = afternoon (after noon). Davāzdah-e zohr = 12 noon.
Asr bekheyr = good afternoon/evening. Emruz asr = this evening (before dark).
Emshab miāy? = are you coming tonight? Em- (this) + shab (night).
Dishab dir khābidam = I slept late last night. Di- (past) + shab, like diruz.
Ba'd az to = after you. Ba'desh chi? = then what? Spoken filler: ba'd migam ... = so then I say ...
Ghabl az zohr = before noon. Ghablan = previously, beforehand. Ghabl az inke beri = before you go.
Mesl-e hamishe = as always. Barāye hamishe = forever.
With negative verb: hargez nemiram = I will never go. Spoken Persian usually says hichvaght instead.
Hichvaght dir nist = it is never too late. Hich (none) + vaght (time).
Hanuz injām = I am still here. Hanuz na = not yet. Hanuz nayumade = he has not come yet.
Do (two) + bāre = a second time. Dobāre begu = say it again. Dobāre shoru' mikonim = we start again.
Zud biā = come quickly/soon. Sobh-e zud = early morning. Zud bāsh! = hurry up!
Dir shod = it got late, I am late. Dir residam = I arrived late. Opposite of zud.
Nān-e tāze = fresh bread. Tāze residam = I just arrived. Tāze chi? = and what is more?
Not a question: vaghti umad, khāb budam = when he came, I was asleep. Vaght (time) + -i.
Ba'dan mibinamet = see you later. Ba'dan behet migam = I will tell you later.
Zamān-e jang = wartime. Ān zamān = back then. Vaght is everyday time; zamān is time as a span or era.
Un mowghe' = at that time. Mowghe'-e shām = dinnertime. Be mowghe' = on time.
In daf'e = this time. Daf'e-ye ba'd = next time. Interchangeable with bār.
Ye bār = once. Chand bār? = how many times? Avvalin bār = the first time. Also: cargo, load.
Boro bālā = go up. Un bālā = up there. Bālā-ye miz = above the table (with ezāfe).
Biā pāyin = come down. Pāyin-e safhe = the bottom of the page.
Berim birun = let us go out. Birun-e shahr = outside the city. Boro birun! = get out!
Dākhel-e otāgh = inside the room. More formal than tu; both are everywhere.
The spoken in: tu khune = at home, tu māshin = in the car. Same spelling as to (you); context decides.
Jelo-ye dar = in front of the door. Biā jelo = come forward. Jelo beshin = sit in front (of the car).
Aghab beshin = sit in the back. Aghab oftādam = I fell behind. Boro aghab = move back.
Kenār-e man beshin = sit next to me. Kenār-e daryā = by the sea. Bezār kenār = set it aside.
Vasat-e shahr = the middle of town. Vasat-e harfam napar = do not jump into the middle of my words.
Nazdik-e khune = near the house. Nazdik bud bioftam = I nearly fell. Opposite: dur.
Dur az injā = far from here. Az dur = from afar. The same spelling read dowr means around: dowr-e miz = around the table.
Miram pish-e doktor = I am going to the doctor. Do sāl pish = two years ago. Pisham bemun = stay with me.
Ketāb ru-ye miz-e = the book is on the table. Spoken: ru miz. Ru be ru = face to face, opposite.
Zir-e miz = under the table. Zir-e bārun = in (under) the rain. Zirzamin = basement, underground.
Posht-e dar = behind the door. Posht-e telefon = on the phone. Posht-e sar = behind (one's head), in one's absence.
Beyn-e mā = between us. Beyn-e khodemun bāshe = keep it between us.
Az un taraf = from that side. Kodum taraf? = which way? Taraf-e man bāsh = be on my side.
Dast-e rāst = the right hand, also: right-hand man. Rāst migi = you are telling the truth. Boro rāst = turn right.
Dast-e chap = the left hand. Bepich be chap = turn left. Chap raft = it went wrong (went left).
Boro mostaghim = go straight, the taxi word. Parvāz-e mostaghim = direct flight.
Chizi mikhāy? = do you want anything? Chizi nist = it is nothing. Chiz + -i (some).
Kasi khune hast? = is anyone home? Kasi nemidune = nobody knows (with negative).
Chi shod? Hichi = what happened? Nothing. Hichi nagoft = he said nothing.
With negative verb: hichkas nayumad = nobody came. Hich + kas (person).
Spoken: dige. Yeki dige = another one. Dige nemiām = I am not coming anymore. Bas-e dige = enough already.
Hamin! = exactly this one. Hamin alān = right this second. Haminjā = right here. Ham (same) + in.
Spoken: hamun. Hamun ke goftam = the very one I said. Hamunjā bemun = stay right there.
Kheyli ziād = very much. Ziād nist = it is not much. Ziādi = excessive, too much.
Ye kam = a little bit. Kam kam = little by little. Kam nayār = do not fall short, hang in there.
Bishtar az sad = more than a hundred. Bishtar-e mardom = most people. Bish (much) + -tar (comparative).
Kamtar az dah = fewer than ten. Kamtar harf bezan = talk less. Kam + -tar.
Behtar az in nemishe = it does not get better than this. Behtari? = feeling better? Beh (good) + -tar.
-tarin makes superlatives from any adjective: bozorgtarin = biggest, badtarin = worst. Behtarin dustam = my best friend.
Khodam = myself, khodet = yourself, khodesh = him/herself. Khodam mikonam = I will do it myself. Khodet ro moarrefi kon = introduce yourself.
Tanhā zendegi mikonam = I live alone. Tanhām nazār = do not leave me alone. Tanhā rāh = the only way.
Ba'zi vaghtā = sometimes. Ba'zi az mardom = some people. Ba'zihā = some folks.
Spoken: hamdige. Hamdige ro mishnāsim = we know each other. Bā hamdige = with one another, together.
Ādam-e khubiye = he is a good person. Ādamā = people (spoken). From the name Adam.
Mardom-e irān = the people of Iran. Mardom chi migan? = what will people say? (the eternal worry).
The counter for people: se nafar = three people. Chand nafarid? = how many are you? (restaurant question).
Stem: khor. Mikhoram = I eat. Chāy mikhori? = will you have tea? Persian eats drinks too: āb khordan = to drink water. Zamin khordan = to fall down.
Stem: khāb. Mikhābam = I sleep. Khābam miād = I am sleepy (my sleep is coming). Note the spelling khvāb with silent v; several khā- words hide one.
Stem: neshin (spoken: shin). Mishinam = I sit. Beshin = sit down. Befarmāid beshinid = please have a seat.
Stem: ist. Spoken favorite: vāisā! = stop, wait! Ist! = halt! Istgāh = station (standing place).

fields.back: to read; to sing; to call
fields.extra: Stem: khān (spoken: khun). Mikhānam / mikhunam = I read. Dars khāndan = to study. Āvāz khāndan = to sing.
Stem: nevis. Minevisam = I write. Benevis = write it down. Neveshte = writing, text.
Stem: shenav (spoken: shno). Mishnavam = I hear. Shenidi? = did you hear? Mishnavi mano? = can you hear me?
Stem: res. Miresam = I arrive. Residim! = we are here! Be mowghe' residam = I arrived on time.
Stem: mān (spoken: mun). Mimānam / mimunam = I stay. Bemun = stay. Chizi namunde = there is nothing left.
Stem: gozār (spoken: zār). Mizāram = I put / I let. Bezār beram = let me go. Bezāresh ru miz = put it on the table.
Stem: gozar. Vaght migzare = time passes. Gozashtehā gozashte = the past is past. One alef away from gozāshtan (to put); mind the pair.
Stem: āvar (spoken: ār). Miāram = I bring. Biār = bring it. Chāy biāram? = shall I bring tea?
Stem: bar. Mibaram = I take / I win. Bordim! = we won! Mano bebar khune = take me home.
Stem: khar. Mikharam = I buy. Chi kharidi? = what did you buy? Kharid = shopping, a purchase.
Stem: forush. Miforusham = I sell. Forushande = seller, shop assistant. Barāye forush = for sale.
Stem: pors. Miporsam = I ask. Ye so'āl beporsam? = may I ask a question? Az man napors = do not ask me.
Stem: fahm. Mifahmam = I understand. Fahmidi? = got it? Nemifahmam = I do not understand, say it early and often.
Stem: shenās. Mishnāsam = I know (him). Mishnāsi mano? = do you know me? Facts use dānestan; people use shenākhtan.
Stem: andāz. Miandāzam = I throw. Bendāz = throw it (spoken). Aks andākhtan = to take a photo.
Stem: oft. Mioftam = I fall. Oftād = it fell. Ettefāgh oftādan = to happen (an event falls).
Stem: dav (spoken: dow). Midavam = I run. Bedo! = run! Nadow = do not run (to a kid).
Stem: bargard. Barmigardam = I will be back. Bargard = come back. Bar (back) + gashtan (to turn).
Stem: ferest. Miferestam = I send. Barām beferest = send it to me (spoken: befres).
Stem: sāz. Misāzam = I build. Sākhtemān = building. Dast-sāz = handmade.
Stem: shekan. Mishkanam = I break (it). Shekast = it broke; as a noun: defeat. Delam ro shekast = he broke my heart.
Stem: band. Mibandam = I close. Dar ro beband = close the door. Baste = closed; also: a package.
Stem: kosh. Mikoshe mano = it is killing me (also said of laughter, cuteness, workload). Koshte-ye in ghazām = I am dying for this food.
Stem: mir. Mimiram barāt = I would die for you (standard affection). Mordam az khande = I died laughing. Morde = dead.
Stem: tars. Mitarsam = I am afraid. Natars = do not be afraid. Az chi mitarsi? = what are you afraid of?
Stem: khand. Mikhandam = I laugh. Nakhand = do not laugh. Behem nakhand = do not laugh at me.
Stem: push. Mipusham = I wear. Chi bepusham? = what should I wear? Lebās pushidan = to get dressed.
Stem: shu(y) (spoken: shur). Mishuyam / mishuram = I wash. Dastāto beshur = wash your hands. Zarf shostan = doing the dishes.
Stem: paz. Mipazam = I cook. Āshpazi = cooking, āshpaz = a cook. Ghazā pokhtan = to cook food.
Stem: bor. Miboram = I cut. Nuno bebor = cut the bread. Boridam = I am worn out (I am cut).

fields.back: to pull; to draw; to take (time)
fields.extra: Stem: kesh. Mikesham = I pull. Naghghāshi keshidan = to draw. Sigār keshidan = to smoke. Tul keshidan = to take long.
Stem: raghs. Miraghsam = I dance. Beraghs! = dance! Raghs = a dance.
Stem: par. Miparam = I jump. Az khāb paridam = I jolted awake. Parande (bird) is family.

fields.back: to turn; to look around for
fields.extra: Stem: gard. Migardam = I turn / I search. Donbālesh migardam = I am looking for it. Bargashtan (return) is its child.
Stem: zan. Mizanam = I hit. The great helper verb: harf zadan = talk, zang zadan = call, dast zadan = touch or clap, ghadam zadan = stroll.
Stem: bakhsh. Bebakhshid = forgive me, the apology you already know. Mibakhshamet = I forgive you.
Fekr mikonam biād = I think he will come. Be to fekr mikonam = I think about you. Compound verbs conjugate only the kardan half.
Kār mikonam = I work. Kojā kār mikoni? = where do you work? Kār nemikone = it does not work (machines too).
Komak mikoni? = will you help? Komakam kon = help me. Komak! = help! Behem komak kard = he helped me.
Fārsi sohbat mikonid? = do you speak Persian? Bā ki sohbat mikonam? = who am I speaking with? (phone).
The casual twin of sohbat kardan: bāhām harf bezan = talk to me. Harf nazan! = stop talking!
Zang (bell) + zadan: behet zang mizanam = I will call you. Zang bezan be māmān = call mom.
Negāh kon! = look! Spoken: negā kon. Film negāh kardan = to watch a movie. Behem negāh nakon = do not look at me.
Gush (ear) + dādan: gush bede = listen. Musighi gush midam = I listen to music. Gush kon also works.
Shoru' konim? = shall we start? Az kojā shoru' konam? = where do I begin? Shoru' shod = it has begun.
Spoken: tamum. Tamum shod = it is over / we ran out. Tamumesh kon = finish it, cut it out.
Dar ro bāz kon = open the door. Bāz = open (adjective) and also: again. Bāz kon lotfan = open up please.
Azash estefāde kon = use it. Mishe az in estefāde konam? = may I use this?
Sabr kon! = wait! Ye lahze sabr konid = one moment please. Sabr = patience, a prized Persian virtue.
Farāmush kardam = I forgot. Farāmushesh kon = forget it. Spoken alternative: yādam raft (it left my memory).
Yād (memory) + gereftan (take): fārsi yād migiram = I am learning Farsi. Zud yād migiri = you learn fast.
Give-to-memory: behem yād bede = teach me. Fārsi yādet midam = I will teach you Farsi.
Literally: to read lesson. Dars mikhunam = I am studying. Kojā dars khundi? = where did you study?
Peydāsh kardam = I found it. Kāret ro peydā kardi? = did you find your thing? Peydā = visible, found.
Kelidam ro gom kardam = I lost my key. Gom shodan = to get lost: gom shodam = I am lost. Gomsho! = get lost!

fields.back: to fix; to make, to prepare
fields.extra: Dorostesh mikonam = I will fix it. Chāy dorost konam? = shall I make tea? Dorost = correct, right.
Lebāsam ro avaz mikonam = I am changing my clothes. Nazaram avaz shod = I changed my mind (it changed).
Sa'y mikonam = I will try. Sa'y kon zud biāy = try to come early. Sa'yam ro mikonam = I will do my best.
Javāb bede = answer! Telefono javāb nemide = he is not answering the phone. Javāb = the answer.
The formal do for tasks: anjāmesh midam = I will get it done. Kāram ro anjām dādam = I finished my task.
Edāme bede = go on, continue. Edāme dāre = to be continued (it has continuation).
Spoken: neshun. Neshunam bede = show me. Behesh neshun dādam = I showed him.
Ejāze bedid = allow me, excuse me (polite interruption). Ejāze hast? = may I? (classroom classic).
Ghol midam = I promise. Ghol dādi! = you promised! Ghol = a promise; zir-e gholet nazan = do not break your word.
Tasmim gereftam bemunam = I decided to stay. Tasmim = decision; tasmim-e sakht = a hard call.
Yeki ro entekhāb kon = pick one. Entekhāb = choice; entekhābāt = elections.
Ta'rif kon! = tell us! (the story). Azat ta'rif kard = he spoke well of you. Che ta'rif! = such praise!
Da'vatet mikonam = I invite you, also: it is my treat. Da'vat = invitation; mehmuni da'vatam = I am invited to a party.
Gerye nakon = do not cry. Zad zir-e gerye = she burst into tears. Gerye = crying.
Dustet dāram = I love you. Chāy dust dāram = I like tea. Literally: to have as friend. Stronger: āsheghetam (I adore you).
Be komak niāz dāram = I need help. Niāz = need; agar niāz bud = if needed. Spoken twin: ehtiāj dāshtan.
Bāvar kon = believe me. Bāvaram nemishe = I cannot believe it. Bāvar = belief.
Hess mikonam khaste-am = I feel tired. Hessesh nist = (I am) not in the mood. Hess = feeling, sense.
Bāham ezdevāj kardand = they got married. Ezdevāj = marriage. The proposal: bā man ezdevāj mikoni?
Safar = trip: safar-e khub! = have a good trip! Ziād safar mikonam = I travel a lot. Safarnāme = travelogue.
Rānandegi baladi? = can you drive? Rānande = driver. Tehrān driving is its own adventure.
Shenā baladam = I know how to swim. Shenā = swimming. Baladi shenā koni? = can you swim?
Har ruz varzesh mikonam = I exercise every day. Varzesh = sport. Varzeshkār = athlete.
Miram kharid = I am going shopping. Kharid dāram = I have shopping to do.
Otāgham ro tamiz kardam = I cleaned my room. Tamiz = clean (adjective).
Literally: to give from the hand. Forsat ro az dast nade = do not miss the chance. Azash dast nakesh = do not give up on it.
Khānevāde-ye man = my family. Khānevādegi = family-style, as a family. The center of Iranian life.
Spoken: barādar or dādāsh (bro). Barādaram = my brother. Same ancient root as English brother.
Spoken: khāhar or ābji (sis). Khāharam = my sister. Another silent v: spelled khvāhar.
Also an exclamation: bābā bikhiāl! = come on, let it go! Ey bābā = oh no, come on.
Māmānam = my mom. Māmān-bozorg = grandma (spoken). From French maman.
Persian splits uncles and aunts four ways. Amu is also friendly address to older men: amu, in chand-e? = uncle, how much is this?
Dāyi jān = dear uncle. The four: amu, dāyi (uncles), amme, khāle (aunts). Each side of the family has its own pair.
Khāle also addresses mom's friends. Pesar-khāle = cousin (khāle's son), also: a too-familiar guy.
Amme-ye man = my (paternal) aunt. Dokhtar-amme = cousin (amme's daughter).
Gender-neutral and polite: ham (same) + sar (head). Hamsaram = my spouse. Used on forms and in polite talk.
Zan o showhar = wife and husband. Showhar kardan = (for a woman) to marry. Spoken: shohar.
Spoken: mehmun. Mehmun dārim = we have guests. Mehmān-navāzi = hospitality, sacred in Iran. Mehmun-e man = my treat.
Hamsāye-ye mā = our neighbor. Ham (same) + sāye (shadow): people who share your shade.
Spoken: jun. Ali jān = dear Ali; māmān jun = dear mom. Jānam? = yes, my dear? (answering a call). Nush-e jān = bon appetit.
Azizam = my dear, sweetheart, said constantly and to almost everyone. Aziz-e man = my dear.
Warmer than dust: rafigham = my buddy. Rafigh! = mate! Loyalty word: rafāghat = true friendship.
Spoken: javun. Javuni = youth (the years). Javun-e khubiye = he is a good young man.
Pir shodam = I have grown old. Pirmard = old man, pirzan = old woman. Things are ghadimi, people are pir.
Badan-e ensān = the human body. Badan-sāzi = bodybuilding (body-making).
Suratesh ro shost = he washed his face. Also: dar har surat = in any case (in every form).
Spoken: dahan. Dahanet ro bāz kon = open your mouth. Dahanam āb oftād = my mouth watered.
Labkhand = smile (lip-laugh). Lab-e daryā = the water's edge. Zir-e lab = under one's breath.
Spoken: dandun. Dandun-dard = toothache. Dandun-pezeshk = dentist. Same root as dental.
Spoken: zabun. Zabān-e fārsi = the Persian language. Zabunam ro gāz gereftam = I bit my tongue.
Gush kon = listen. Gusham bā to-e = I am listening (my ear is with you). Gushvāre = earring.
Spoken Persian says damāgh. Bini is the written/medical word; Tehran is famously the nose-job capital.
Muhām = my hair (plural). Mu be mu = in exact detail (hair by hair). Barber: ārāyeshgar.
Gardan-band = necklace. Gardanam dard mikone = my neck hurts. Gardan gereftan = to take the blame.
Shekamam dard mikone = my stomach hurts. Shekamu = glutton, foodie (affectionate).
Pāsho! = get up! (on your feet). Piāde = on foot. Pā-ye man = my leg. Same root as pedal, pedestrian.
Angoshtar = ring (finger thing). Angosht-e shast = thumb. Panj angosht = five fingers.
Ghalbam tond mizane = my heart is racing. For feelings Persian says del; the muscle is ghalb.
Khun-e man = my blood. Ham-khun = blood relative. Khunam be jush umad = my blood boiled.
Pust-e sib = apple peel. Pustam khoshk shode = my skin has gotten dry.
Sedāt ro nemishnavam = I cannot hear your voice. Sedām kon = call me (make my sound). Bā sedā-ye boland = out loud.
Otāgh-e khāb = bedroom (sleep room). Otāgh dārid? = do you have a room? (hotel).
Āshpaz (cook) + khāne (house). Spoken: āshpazkhune. Where every Iranian gathering actually happens.
Spoken: hammum. Miram hammum = I am going to shower. The toilet is dastshuyi, a separate room in Iranian homes.
The traveler's word: dastshuyi kojāst? = where is the restroom? Literally: hand-washing place.
Takht-e khāb = bed. Ru takht derāz keshidam = I lay down on the bed. Also: throne.
Ru-ye miz = on the table. Miz ro bechin = set the table. Miz-e kār = desk.
Ru sandali beshin = sit on the chair. Sandali-ye jelo = the front seat.
Panjere ro bāz kon = open the window. Kenār-e panjere = by the window.
Divār ke mush dāre, mush ham gush dāre = walls have mice and mice have ears (walls have ears).
Kelidam ro gom kardam = I lost my key. Also the light switch: kelid-e bargh. From Greek, cousin of clé.
The Persian carpet: farsh-e irāni. Ru-ye farsh = on the carpet. Homes are shoe-free; the farsh is sacred ground.
Cherāgh ro roshan kon = turn on the light. Cherāgh-e ghermez = red (traffic) light.
Telefon zang mizane = the phone is ringing. Shomāre telefon = phone number.
From gush (ear): the thing at your ear. Gushim kojāst? = where is my phone? Gushi ro bardār = pick up.
Televizion mibinam = I watch TV. Spoken: telviziyon or just tivi.
Yakh (ice) + chāl (pit): the ancient ice-pit became the fridge. Bezāresh tu yakhchāl = put it in the fridge.
Zarfhā ro mishuram = I will do the dishes. Zarf-e ghazā = food container.
Ye livān āb = a glass of water. Livān-e chāy = a cup of tea (though tea prefers the estekān).
The waisted little glass Persian tea lives in, with a ghand (sugar cube) held between the teeth. Ye estekān chāy = one glass of tea.
Ghāshogh o changāl = spoon and fork, the standard Iranian cutlery pair; rice is eaten with the spoon.
Changāl ro bede = pass the fork. From chang, claw.
Chāghu tiz-e = the knife is sharp. Chāghu daste-ye khodesh ro nemibore = a knife does not cut its own handle (people protect their own).
Boshghābet ro tamum kon = finish your plate. Boshghāb-parande = flying saucer.
Kif-e pul = wallet (money bag). Kif dāri? = do you have your bag? Also: kif kardam = I loved it, what a delight.
Ketāb khāndan = to read (books). Ketāb-forushi = bookshop. From the same root family as maktab and kitab across the region.
Daftar-e telefon = phone book. Daftar-e kār = the office. Daftarche = booklet.
Ye tekke kāghaz = a piece of paper. Kāghaz-bāzi = paperwork, red tape (paper-games).
Ghalam dāri? = got a pen? Also a calligrapher's reed. Khodkār = ballpoint, the everyday word for pen.
Aks begiram? = shall I take a photo? Aks-e yādegāri = souvenir photo. Akkās = photographer.
Shahr-e Tehrān = the city of Tehran. Markaz-e shahr = downtown. Hamshahri = fellow townsman.
Keshvar-e irān = the country of Iran. Khārej az keshvar = abroad (outside the country).
Hame jā-ye donyā = everywhere in the world. Donyā kuchike = it is a small world. Ye donyā mamnun = a world of thanks.
Zamin khordam = I fell down (ate ground). Kore-ye zamin = planet Earth. Zamin-e futbāl = football pitch.
Rustāhā-ye irān = the villages of Iran. Rustāyi = villager, rural. Spoken also: deh.
Spoken: khiābun. Khiābun-e Vali-asr: Tehran's 18 km avenue, the longest in the Middle East. Sar-e khiābun = at the corner.
The narrow lanes of old neighborhoods. Kuche-ye bombast = dead-end alley. Kuche be kuche = lane by lane.
Spoken: meydun. Meydun-e āzādi = Azadi Square, Tehran's landmark. Also a battlefield or arena.
Pol-e khāju in Esfahan: a bridge people sing under. Az pol rad sho = cross the bridge.
Maghāze baste-ast = the shop is closed. Maghāzedār = shopkeeper.
The word English borrowed. Bāzār-e bozorg-e Tehrān = the Grand Bazaar. Bāzāri = merchant class.
Miram bānk = I am going to the bank. Kārt-e bānki = bank card.
Bimār (sick) + -estān (place of): the place of the sick, same -stan as in Afghanistan. Bebarinesh bimārestān = take him to the hospital.
Dāru (medicine) + khāne (house). Dārukhāne-ye shabāneruzi = 24-hour pharmacy, a lifesaver phrase.
Miram madrese = I go to school. Modir-e madrese = the principal.
Dānesh (knowledge) + gāh (place). Dāneshgāh-e Tehrān = University of Tehran. Dāneshju = university student.
Ketāb + khāne = book house. Tu ketābkhāne dars mikhunam = I study in the library.
Masjed-e jāme' = the main (Friday) mosque of a town. The English word mosque descends from masjed.
Berim pārk = let us go to the park. Pārk kardan = to park a car; both meanings imported.
Resturān-e irāni = Persian restaurant. Menu classics: kabāb, ghorme-sabzi, gheyme.
Berim kāfe = let us go to a cafe. Tehran cafe culture is huge. Ghahve-khāne = the traditional teahouse.
Hotel gereftam = I booked a hotel. Otāgh-e ye nafare = single room.
Forud (landing) + gāh (place). Miram forudgāh = I am off to the airport.
Ist (stand) + gāh (place): where things stand. Istgāh-e metro = metro station. Istgāh-e ba'di = next stop.
Berim sinemā = let us go to the movies. Sinemā-ye irān = Iranian cinema, an art-house superpower.
Muze-ye melli = the National Museum. Muze-ye farsh = the Carpet Museum, yes it exists.
Ghazā khordi? = have you eaten? Ghazā-ye irāni = Persian food. Ghazā hāzere = the food is ready.
Spoken: nun. Fresh from the bakery (nunvāyi): sangak, barbari, lavāsh, tāftun. Nun o panir = bread and cheese, the default breakfast.
Iran's staple. Berenj-e irāni = Iranian rice, a point of national pride. Cooked and fluffed it becomes polo.
The crown of the table: polo bā kabāb. The crispy golden crust at the bottom, tahdig, is fought over.
Kabāb kubide (minced) and kabāb barg (fillet) over rice. Jujeh kabāb = grilled chicken. The word went global from here.
Gusht-e gāv = beef, gusht-e gusfand = lamb. Bi gusht = without meat (useful for vegetarians).
Morgh o polo = chicken and rice. Tokhm-e morgh = egg. Zereshk polo bā morgh: the wedding classic.
Sabzi polo bā māhi = herbed rice with fish, the Nowruz eve dinner. Māhigir = fisherman.
Literally: chicken seed. Nimru = fried egg. Tokhm-e morgh-e āb-paz = boiled egg.
Nun o panir o sabzi = bread, white cheese, and fresh herbs: the eternal Iranian breakfast spread.
On the table at every meal. Māst o khiār = yogurt with cucumber and mint. Borāni = yogurt sides.
One spelling, three words: drink the first, respect the second, turn the third (shir-e āb = water tap). Context always tells you.
Nun o kare o asal = bread, butter, honey. Same spelling as kore (globe): kore-ye zamin = planet Earth.
From sabz (green). The plate of fresh herbs (sabzi khordan) beside every meal, and the herb stew ghorme sabzi.
The fruit bowl is Iranian hospitality's front line; refusing is ta'ārof, so take one. Āb-mive = juice.
Sib-e sorkh = red apple. Sits on the Nowruz haft-sin table. Sibzamini (earth apple) = potato.
Iran's fruit: eaten with salt, juiced (āb-anār), reduced into fesenjān stew. Shab-e Yaldā (winter solstice) is anār night.
Named after Portugal, whose traders carried it. Āb-portoghāl = orange juice. The color orange is nāranji.
The start of nearly every Persian stew. Piāz dāgh = crispy fried onions, the finishing touch.
Same spelling twice: sir dāgh = fried garlic; siram = I am full, the word you need at an Iranian table where refills never stop.
Literally: foreign plum. Grilled beside every kabāb. Spoken: goje. Goje kharidam = I bought tomatoes.
Earth apple, like French pomme de terre. Sib-zamini sorkh-karde = fries.
Namak dāre = it is charming (has salt); bā-namak = cute, funny. Bi-namak = bland, of food and of people.
English sugar traces back through Arabic and Persian shekar. Chāy bā shekar? = tea with sugar?
Tea ritual: park the ghand between your teeth and sip the chāy through it. Ghand tu delam āb shod = I melted with joy.
Iran grows most of the world's saffron. Za'ferāni rice crowns the dish; the English word saffron came west through za'farān.
The national drink, drunk dark from the samāvar in estekān glasses, morning to midnight. Ye chāy bede = pour me a tea.
Ghahve-ye tork = Turkish coffee, read for fortunes (fāl-e ghahve). The teahouse is ghahve-khāne, yet serves tea.
Salty, minty, sometimes fizzy: the kabāb's official companion. An acquired love; acquire it.
Āsh-e reshte: noodle and bean āsh ladled out on rainy days and religious holidays. Āshpaz (cook) literally means āsh-maker.
From shirin (sweet). Shirini gerefti? = did you bring pastries? Good news obligates you: shirini bede! = treat us!
Bastani sonnati: saffron ice cream with frozen cream chips and rosewater. Summer word number one.
Sobh (morning) + -āne. Sobhāne khordi? = did you have breakfast? Bread, cheese, herbs, tea.
The main meal of the Iranian day, ideally followed by a nap. Nāhār chi dārim? = what is for lunch?
Shām bemun = stay for dinner (you will be asked; it is not entirely optional). Ba'd az shām = after dinner.
Spoken: goshne. Goshname = I am hungry (goshna-am). Az goshnegi mordam = I am starving.
Teshname = I am thirsty. Teshne-ye yād gereftan = thirsty to learn.
Khosh (pleasant) + maze (taste). The compliment every cook is waiting for: kheyli khoshmaze bud!
Che rangi? = what color? Rang kardan = to paint or to dupe someone. Rangārang = colorful.
Nun-e sangak o panir-e sefid = flatbread and white cheese. Rish-sefid = elder, white-beard (the respected mediator).
Spoken usually meshki for objects: lebās-e meshki = black clothes. Siāh o sefid = black and white.
Cherāgh ghermez = red light. From the crimson insect dye; English crimson shares the root.
From āb, water: water-colored. Āsemun-e ābi = blue sky. Kāshi-ye ābi = the blue tiles of Esfahan.
Sabz mesl-e bahār = green like spring. Fazā-ye sabz = green space. Sabzi (herbs) is its child.
Zard mesl-e za'ferān = yellow as saffron. Payiz-e zard = the yellow autumn.
Coffee-colored: ghahve + -i, exactly like English coffee-colored. Cheshmā-ye ghahve-i = brown eyes.
Havā garme = the weather is hot. Garm o samimi = warm and friendly. Same ancient root as English warm.
Āb-e sard = cold water. Havā sard shod = it got cold. Sard shod = (the food) went cold, eat!

fields.back: scalding hot; fresh off the fire
fields.extra: Chāy-e dāgh = piping hot tea. Khabar-e dāgh = hot news. For weather use garm; dāgh is touch-hot.
Khis shodam = I got soaked. Zir-e bārun khis nasho = do not get wet in the rain.
Mive-ye khoshk = dried fruit. Khoshk-shuyi = dry cleaner. Also stiff, rigid: ādam-e khoshk.
Dastāt kasife = your hands are dirty. Kār-e kasif = dirty business.
Por az āb = full of water. Por-kār = hardworking (full of work). Del-e por = a heart full (of grievance).
Otāgh-e khāli = an empty room. Jāt khāli = your place was empty: we missed you, the classic after any gathering.
From sang, stone: stone-like. Kif-e sangin = a heavy bag. Also dignified: ādam-e sangin.
Bār-e sabok = a light load. Also flighty, undignified: sabok nasho = do not cheapen yourself.
Fārsi sakht nist! = Persian is not hard! Sakht nagir = take it easy, do not make it hard.
Spoken: āsun. Kār-e āsuni nist = it is no easy task. Āsān is the written word; everyday speech usually reaches for rāhat.
Rāhat bāsh = make yourself comfortable, relax. Khiālam rāhat shod = my mind is at ease. Rāhat-tar = easier.
Sari' biā = come quickly. Ghatār-e sari' = express train. Spoken twin: tond.
Tond naro = do not go so fast. Ghazā-ye tond = spicy food. Tond nasho = do not snap at me.
Āheste boro = go slowly. Spoken twin: yavāsh. Yavāsh yavāsh = little by little, gently does it.
Ādam-e ghavi = a strong person. Chāy-e ghavi = strong tea. Ghavi bāsh = be strong.
Noghte-ye za'f = weak point. Interneteshun za'ife = their internet is weak.
Boland sho = get up. Sedā-ye boland = loud voice. Mu-ye boland = long hair. Boland-parvāz = ambitious (high-flying).
Spoken: kutā. Mu-ye kutāh = short hair. Dāstān-e kutāh = short story. Kutāh biā = back down, let it go.
Chāgh shodam = I have gained weight (the post-holiday sentence). Historically a compliment; ask an old grandmother.
Lāghar shodi! = you have lost weight! (said approvingly or worriedly, depending on the aunt).
The written, poetic word: shahr-e zibā = a beautiful city. Speech says khoshgel or ghashang.
Khosh (pleasant) + gel (clay, mold): well-formed, of good clay. Che khoshgel! = how pretty! Khoshgele = she is gorgeous.
Zeshte! = that is rude, unseemly! (about behavior more often than faces).
Māshin-e jadid = a new car. Chi khabar-e jadid? = any news? Sāl-e jadid = the new year.
Dust-e ghadimi = an old friend. Khune-ye ghadimi = an old house. People are pir, things are ghadimi.
Lebās-e kohne = worn-out clothes. Ghadimi is vintage with dignity; kohne is just old. Zakhm-e kohne = an old wound.
Spoken: arzun. Arzun kharidam = I got it cheap. Arzun-tar nemishe? = can it be cheaper? (bazaar essential).
Spoken: gerun. Kheyli gerune! = that is so expensive! (say it in the bazaar even when it is not). Gerāni = inflation, expensiveness.
Mohem nist = it does not matter. Chiz-e mohemmi nist = it is nothing important. Mohemtarin = the most important.
Ghazā-ye sāde = simple food. Ādam-e sāde = a straightforward (or naive) person. Chāy-e sāde = tea, no sugar.
Che ajib! = how strange! Ajib gharib = weird and wonderful. Ādam-e ajibiye = he is an odd one.
Resturān-e ma'ruf = a famous restaurant. Be in ma'rufe ke ... = it is famous for ...
Khatar (danger) + -nāk (full of). Khatarnāke! = it is dangerous! Khatar = danger itself.
Jā-ye amn = a safe place. Irān barāye tourist amne? = is Iran safe for tourists? (Yes, famously hospitable.)
Ghalate = it is wrong. Ghalat kardam = I was dead wrong, I take it back (strong apology). Dorost o ghalat = right and wrong.
Ādam-e khās = someone special. Chiz-e khāssi nist = nothing special. Khās o ām = everyone (elite and common).
Āzādi = freedom, and the name of Tehran's tower and square. Vaght-e āzād = free time. Free of charge is majjāni or rāygān.
Sāket! = quiet! Sāket bāsh = be quiet. Jā-ye sāket = a quiet spot. Sokut = silence.
Tehrān shulughe = Tehran is hectic. Sarām shulughe = I am swamped. Shulugh nakon = do not make a scene.
Cherāgh roshane = the light is on. Roshan kon = turn it on. Roshan shod? = is it clear now? Rowshan-fekr = intellectual (bright-minded).
Otāgh-e tārik = a dark room. Havā tārik shod = it got dark. Tāriki = darkness.
Khosh (pleasant) + hāl (state). Khoshhālam = I am happy. Az didanet khoshhāl shodam = pleased to meet you.
Nā- (un-) + rāhat (at ease). Nārāhat nabāsh = do not be upset. Nārāhat shodi? = did I upset you?
Asabāni nasho = do not get angry. Az dastet asabāniyam = I am angry at you (from your hand).
Khaste-am = I am tired. The national greeting to anyone working: khaste nabāshid = may you not be tired.
Negarān nabāsh = do not worry. Negarānet shodam = I got worried about you. Negarāni = worry.
Az tars = out of fear. Tars nadāre = there is nothing to fear. Tarsidan is its verb; tarsu = scaredy-cat.
The grand word of Persian poetry. Eshgh-e man = my love. Eshghame = (s)he is my everything (spoken).
Āsheghet shodam = I fell in love with you. Āshegh-e in āhangam = I adore this song. Ma'shugh = the beloved.
Be omid-e didār = hoping to see you again (a formal goodbye). Omidvāram = I hope (so). Nā-omid = hopeless.
Ārezu mikonam movaffagh beshi = I wish you success. Ārezu-ye man ine ke ... = my dream is that ...
The other pole of Persian song. Gham nakhor = do not grieve, do not worry (do not eat sorrow).
Ghamgin be nazar miresi = you look sad. Āhang-e ghamgin = a sad song (Persian playlists overflow with them).
Khande-dār = funny (laughter-bearing). Khandam gereft = I got the giggles. Az khande mordim = we died laughing.
Lab (lip) + khand (laugh): a lip-laugh. Labkhand bezan = smile!
Hālet chetore? = how are you? Hālam khube = I am fine. Hāl nadāram = I am not feeling it. Hāl kardam = I loved it (slang).
Untranslatable essential: hosele nadāram = I cannot be bothered, I do not have the (mental) energy. Hoselam sar raft = I am bored stiff.
Ajale dāram = I am in a hurry. Ajale nakon = do not rush. Kār-e ajale-i = a rush job.
Spoken: ārum. Ārum bāsh = calm down. Ārum ārum = gently, bit by bit. Ārāmesh = peace of mind.
Asabiyam = my nerves are shot, I am wound up. Asab = nerve; a'sābam khord shod = my nerves got crushed.
Khejālat nakesh = do not be shy. Khejālat bekesh! = shame on you! Khejālati = a shy person.
Khosh + bakht (luck, fortune). Khoshbakhtam = pleased to meet you (I am fortunate). Khoshbakhti = happiness, good fortune.
Bad + bakht: ill-fated. Badbakht shodam = I am ruined (usually theatrical). Also pity: bichāre, badbakht = poor thing.
Be nazaram = in my opinion. Nazaret chiye? = what do you think? Nazar dādan = to give an opinion.
Yādam hast = I remember. Yādam raft = I forgot (it left my memory). Yādet bāshe = remember this. Be yād-e to = in memory of you.
Khābam miād = I am sleepy. Khāb didam = I had a dream (saw a sleep). Khāb-ālu = drowsy. Silent v again: khvāb.
Khiālat rāhat = rest easy, do not worry. Bi-khiāl = never mind, forget it (the great Iranian shrug). Khiāl kardam = I assumed.
Aghlet kojā rafte? = where has your sense gone? Āghel = sensible. Aghlāni = rational.
Spoken: divune. Divune shodi? = have you lost it? Divune-ye to-am = I am crazy about you. From div, demon: demon-touched.
Ahmagh nabāsh = do not be a fool. Kār-e ahmaghāne = a foolish deed. Harsh word; aim it at deeds, not hosts.
Bā (with) + hush (intelligence). Bachche-ye bāhush = a bright kid. Hush-e masnu'i = artificial intelligence.
Spoken: mehrabun. From mehr, the old Persian word for love and for the sun (and the month Mehr). Kheyli mehrabuni = you are so kind.
So'āl dāram = I have a question. So'āl kardan / porsidan = to ask. Javāb-e so'āl = the answer to the question.
Dorugh nagu! = do not lie! (also: no way, really?). Dorugh-gu = liar. Dorugh-e maslahati = a white lie (expedient lie).
Haghighat ro begu = tell the truth. Dar haghighat = in truth, actually. Haghighi = real, true.
Dāstān-e kutāh = short story. Dāstān chiye? = what is the story here, what is going on? Dāstān darāz-e = it is a long story.
Che khabar? = what is new? (the second sentence of every conversation). Bi-khabar = unannounced; azash khabar nadāram = I have not heard from him.
Kalame be kalame = word for word. Ye kalame ham nagoft = he did not say a single word.
Ye jomle begu = say one sentence. Jomle-sāzi = sentence-building, exactly what you are training now.
Ya'ni chi? = what does it mean? (its cousin ya'ni = it means). Ma'ni-ye in kalame chiye? = what does this word mean? Bā-ma'ni = meaningful.
Dars khāndan = to study. Dars-e avval = lesson one. Behesh dars dādam = I taught him a lesson.
Mo'allem-e madrese = schoolteacher. Ruz-e mo'allem = Teacher's Day. From elm, knowledge.
University professors and master craftsmen alike: ostād! Also sincere praise: ostādi! = masterful!
Dānesh (knowledge) + ju (seeker): knowledge-seeker. Dāneshju-ye pezeshki = medical student.
Emtehān dāram = I have an exam. Emtehān kon = try it, test it. Emtehānesh majjāniye = trying costs nothing.
Nāme neveshtan = to write a letter. Barnāme = program (plan-letter). -nāme builds documents: shenāsnāme = ID, gozarnāme = passport.
Shomāre-ye telefonet chande? = what is your phone number? Shomāre gereftan = to dial.
Fārsi baladi? = do you know Farsi? Fārsi-ye man khub nist, hanuz = my Farsi is not good, yet. Say it proudly and badly; doors open.
Englisi baladid? = do you speak English? Be englisi chi mishe? = how do you say it in English?
Land of the Aryans, the name locals have always used for Persia. Irān raftam = I went to Iran. Zende bād irān = long live Iran.
Man irāni-am = I am Iranian. Ghazā-ye irāni = Persian food. -i turns places into peoples: tehrāni, esfahāni.
Film didan = to watch a movie. Film-e irāni = Iranian film; start with Kiarostami or Farhadi. Film bāzi nakon = stop acting, quit the drama.
Musighi-ye sonnati = traditional Persian music: the setār, the santur, the voice of Shajarian. Gush dādan be musighi = listening to music.
In āhang ro dust dāram = I love this song. Āhang-sāz = composer. Pish-āhang = the lead, pioneer.
Honarmand = artist (one who has honar). Honar nazd-e irāniān ast o bas: art belongs to the Iranians alone, the (tongue-in-cheek) national boast.
Persians settle arguments with Hafez and grief with Rumi. She'r goftan = to compose verse. Shā'er = poet.
Shoghlet chiye? = what do you do? Shoghl-e āzād = self-employed (free occupation), the classic form answer.
Edāre-ye post = the post office. Miram edāre = I am off to the office. Edāre kardan = to manage, run.
Sherkat-e khosusi = private company. Sherkat kardan = to participate (to company-in). Tu sherkat kār mikonam = I work at a company.
Modir-e sherkat = the company manager. Modir-e madrese = school principal. Modiriat = management.
Spoken: re'is. Ra'is-e man = my boss. Ra'is-jomhur = president (head of the republic). Khodet ra'isi = you are the boss.
Kār + -mand (possessor of): one who has work. Kārmand-e bānk = bank employee.
Kār + -gar (doer): one who does work. Ruz-e kāregar = Labor Day. Kāregar-e sākhtemān = construction worker.
Two lives: hoghugham kame = my salary is small; hoghugh-e bashar = human rights. Plural of hagh (right).
Gheymatesh chande? = what is the price? Gerun-gheymat = pricey. Bi-gheymat... say arzun instead.
Hesāb, lotfan = the bill, please. Hesāb-e bānki = bank account. Hesābesh kon = figure it, count it. Bi-hesāb = beyond counting.
Kārt mikeshid? = do you take card? Kārt-e melli = national ID card. Kārt be kārt = a bank transfer (card to card), how everyone pays everyone.
Spoken: tomen. Prices are quoted in toman = 10 rial, though bills print rial: the eternal tourist trap. Chand toman? = how many toman?
Bāyad beri doktor = you should see a doctor. Āghā-ye doktor / khānom-e doktor: the honorific of anyone with a doctorate.
Parastār-e bimārestān = hospital nurse. From parastāri, caregiving. Ruz-e parastār = Nurses' Day.
The prestige title: āghā-ye mohandes! greets anyone who looks technical. Mohandes-e narmafzār = software engineer.
Vakil gereftam = I hired a lawyer. Vakil-e man = my attorney. Vekālat = power of attorney, the law profession.
Polis khabar kon = call the police. Polis-e rāhnamāyi = traffic police, whom Tehran drivers regard as advisory.
Sar (head) + bāz (gambler): one who wagers his head. Sarbāzi = the mandatory military service every Iranian man debates dodging.
Zir-e derakht = under the tree. Derakht-e sib = apple tree. Old riddle-image of family trees and shade for guests.
Ye daste gol = a bouquet. Gol alone often means the rose, king of Persian gardens. Also a football goal: gol zad! = he scored!
Tehran leans on the Alborz: kuh-e Damāvand is the mythic peak. Miram kuh = I am going hiking (to the mountain).
Daryā-ye khazar = the Caspian. Mesl-e daryā = vast and generous, like the sea (a compliment).
Daryā + -che (little): a little sea. Daryāche-ye Urumiye = Lake Urmia.
Rud (river) + khāne (house). Kenār-e rudkhāne = by the river. Zāyande-rud waters Esfahan.
The lush north (shomāl) is jangal country. Persian jangal and English jungle are cousins, both from Sanskrit via Hindi.
Bi (without) + āb (water) + -ān: the waterless place. The Dasht-e Kavir and Lut deserts fill Iran's heart.
Spoken: āsemun. Āsemun-e ābi = blue sky. Setāre-hā-ye āsemun = the stars of the sky.
Tolu'-e khorshid = sunrise. The lion-and-sun (shir o khorshid) is old Iran's emblem.
The sun as felt: āftāb dāre = it is sunny. Jelo-ye āftāb nashin = do not sit in the sun. Āftāb-gardun = sunflower.
Same ancient root as English star. Setāre-ye sinemā = film star.
Havā abriye = it is cloudy. Abr o bād o mah o khorshid: clouds, wind, moon and sun, all at work (a beloved Saadi line).
Spoken: bārun. Bārun miād = it is raining. Zir-e bārun ghadam zadan = walking in the rain, a national romantic pastime.
Barf miād = it is snowing. Ādam-barfi = snowman (snow-person). Tehran winters deliver.
Bād miād = it is windy. Bādkonak = balloon. Bād āvorde ro bād mibare = easy come, easy go (what wind brings, wind takes).
Havā chetore? = how is the weather? Havā-ye tāze = fresh air. Havāto dāram = I have got your back (I hold your air).
Spoken: ātish. Ātish gereft = it caught fire. Chahārshanbe-suri: jumping over fires before Nowruz.
Del-e sang = a heart of stone. Sang tamum gozāshtan = to go all out (lay the full stone).
Khāk-e irān = the soil of Iran, the homeland word. Khāki = dusty, earth-colored; also humble, down-to-earth.
Ruz-e tabi'at: the 13th day of Nowruz, spent picnicking outdoors. Tabi'at-gardi = nature-touring.
The year begins at the spring equinox with Nowruz. Bahār-nārenj = orange blossom, scent of Shiraz.
Spoken: tābestun. Ta'tilāt-e tābestun = summer holidays. Garmā-ye tābestun = summer heat.
Barg-rizān-e pāyiz = autumn's leaf-fall. Madrese-hā pāyiz bāz mishan = schools open in fall.
Spoken: zemestun. Yaldā, the longest night, opens winter. Zemestun-e sard = a cold winter.
Chahār fasl = four seasons; Iran brags it has all four at once. Fasl-e avval = chapter one.
Spoken: heyvun. Heyvun-e khānegi = pet (house animal). Bāgh-e vahsh = zoo (wild garden).
Sag-e man = my dog. Khaste mesl-e sag = dog-tired. Sag bā-vafāst = dogs are loyal, the standard line about them.
The Persian cat: gorbe-ye irāni. Iran's map is affectionately called the cat. Gorbe-ye man = my cat.
Asb-savāri = horseback riding. The Persian horse carried an empire; the word rides in asb-e ābi = hippo (water horse).
From paridan, to fly: the flying one. Parande-hā sobh mikhunand = the birds sing at morning.
Gusht-e gāv = beef. Shir-e gāv = cow's milk. Same ancient root as English cow.
Gusht-e gusfand = lamb, the kabāb gold standard. Chupān = shepherd.
Bā māshin miram = I go by car. Māshin dāri? = do you have a car? Māshin-e lebās-shuyi = washing machine.
Bā otobus = by bus. Istgāh-e otobus = bus stop. Otobus-e shab = the night bus.
Tāksi! Darbast? = taxi! private hire? (darbast = doors-closed, no shared stops). Snapp is the local Uber.
Metro-ye tehrān: cheap, packed, and the fastest thing in the city. Kārt-e metro = metro card.
Bā ghatār be mashhad = to Mashhad by train, the classic overnight ride. Istgāh-e ghatār = train station.
Havā (air) + peymā (traverser): the air-crosser. Bilit-e havāpeymā = plane ticket.
Do (two) + charkh (wheel) + -e: the two-wheeler. Docharkhe-savāri = cycling.
Tehran's weaving motorbikes are half the traffic. Motori = motorbike courier. Also the engine: motor-e māshin.
Kashti-ye nuh = Noah's ark. Same spelling as koshti, wrestling, Iran's ancient national sport; context (or vowels) decide.
From French billet. Belit gereftam = I got tickets. Belit-e ghatār = train ticket.
Mosāfer dāram = I have guests from out of town. Mosāferat = travel; safar is its root.
Spoken: chamedun. Chamedunam ro bastam = I packed my bag. From Russian chemodan.
Pāsportet hamrāhet bāshe = keep your passport on you. The formal word: gozarnāme (pass-letter).
Jādde-ye chālus: the mountain road to the Caspian, gorgeous and jammed. Khiābān is in town; jādde is out of town.
Terāfik-e tehrān = Tehran traffic, the city's defining epic. Tu terāfik gir kardam = I am stuck in traffic.
From German Benzin. Pomp-e benzin = gas station. Benzin tamum kardim = we ran out of gas.
Lebās pushidan = to get dressed. Lebās-e now barāye eyd = new clothes for the new year.
Spoken: pirhan. Pirāhan-e sefid = a white shirt. Covers both men's shirts and women's dresses.
Shalvār-e jin = jeans. Kot o shalvār = suit (jacket and trousers). English pyjamas = Persian pā-jāme, leg-garment.
Kafshāto dar biār = take off your shoes (always, at every Iranian door). Kafsh-e varzeshi = sneakers.
Ye joft jurāb = a pair of socks. You will be padding around homes in them; bring your good ones.
Kolāh saret nazāran = do not let them put a hat on your head (rip you off). Kolāh-bardāri = fraud.
Kot o shalvār = a suit. Kotam ro biāram? = should I grab my jacket?
Ru (on) + sar (head) + -i: the on-the-head thing. Everyday word in Iranian dress code talk.
Tu jibam = in my pocket. Az jib-e khodam = out of my own pocket. Jib-bor = pickpocket (pocket-cutter).
Eynak mizanam = I wear glasses. Eynak-e āftābi = sunglasses (sun glasses). Eynaki = the one with glasses.
Miz-e chubi = wooden table. Ye tekke chub = a piece of wood. Chub khordan = to take a beating.
Rāh-āhan = railway (iron road). Mesl-e āhan = iron-hard. Āhangar = blacksmith.
Angoshtar-e talā = gold ring. Talā-forushi = jewelry shop, glittering whole streets of the bazaar.
Zarf-e noghre = silverware. Noghre-i = silver-colored, also a silvery car.
Shishe-ye panjere = window glass. Ye shishe āb = a bottle of water. Shekastani mesl-e shishe = fragile as glass.
Marizam = I am sick. Mariz shodi? = did you get sick? The formal word is bimār, as in bimārestān (hospital).
Sar-dard = headache, del-dard = stomachache, dandun-dard = toothache. Dardet chiye? = what is bothering you?
Dāru mikhoram = I take medicine (Persian eats medicine). Dārukhāne = pharmacy.
Ghors-e sardard = headache pill. Ruzi do tā ghors = two pills a day. Also: firm, solid (ghors o mohkam).
Tab dāram = I have a fever. Tab kard = he came down with a fever. Tab-e futbāl = football fever.
Sarmā khordam = I caught a cold (I ate the cold). Sarmā-ye zemestun = the winter cold.
Be salāmati! = to (your) health, the universal toast. Salāmat bāshid = be well, the blessing after a favor.
Zakhmi shod = he got wounded. Zakhm-e zabān = the wound words make (tongue-wound).
Amal kardan = to operate, or to act on a promise. Amalan = in practice. Mard-e amal = a man of action.
Āzmāyesh-e khun = blood test. Āzmāyeshgāh = laboratory. Āzmāyesh kardan = to test, to try out.
Dard o darmān = the ailment and its cure. Darmāngāh = clinic. Bi-darmān = incurable.
Marg o zendegi = life and death. Mas'ale-ye marg o zendegi-e = it is a matter of life and death. From the same ancient root as mordan, to die.
Tavallodet mobārak = happy birthday (the song too). Ruz-e tavallod = birthday. Motevalled-e 1370 = born in 1370 (Iranian calendar).
Jashn gereftan = to throw a celebration. Jashn-e nowruz = the New Year festival. Jashnvāre = festival (of film, of arts).
From arus, bride. Iranian weddings: hundreds of guests, dancing, and the bride and groom dipping pinkies in honey.
Spoken: mehmuni. Mehmuni davatam = I am invited to a party. Home parties are the beating heart of Iranian social life.
Hediye gereftam barāt = I got you a present. Spoken also: kādo (from French cadeau). In hediye ast = it is a gift.
Arus khānom = the bride. Both the bride at the wedding and the wife she becomes to her in-laws.
Arus o dāmād = bride and groom. Āghā dāmād = the groom (as the wedding crowd calls him).
Stem: shomār (spoken: shmor). Mishmoram = I count. Beshmor: yek, do, se ... = count: one, two, three ...
Stem: pardāz. Mipardāzam = I pay. Everyday speech prefers pul dādan or hesāb kardan: man hesāb mikonam = I will pay (the bill).

fields.back: to turn, to spin, to wander
fields.extra: Stem: charkh (wheel). Micharkhe = it spins. Tu shahr charkhidim = we wandered around town.
Spoken: tekun. Tekunesh nade = do not move it. Tekun bokhor! = get moving! Dast tekun dād = he waved (shook his hand).

fields.back: to deliver; to give a ride
fields.extra: Stem: rasān (spoken: resun). Miresunamet = I will drop you off. Berasun be dastash = get it to him. Residan's causative twin.
Stem: bardār. Bardār = pick it up. Gushi ro bardār = answer the phone. Bar (up) + dāshtan (have).
Da'vā nakonid = do not fight. Da'vāmun shod = we had a fight. Da'vā = a quarrel or brawl.
Stem: dozd, which is also the noun: thief. Kifam ro dozdidan = they stole my bag. Dozd! = thief!
Stem: bus. Mibusamet = kisses to you (how calls and texts end). Bus = a kiss; māch = the smacking spoken version.
Baghalam kon = hug me. Baghal = embrace, arms; bachche ro baghal kard = she took the child in her arms.
Māshinam ta'mir mikhād = my car needs fixing. Ta'mirgāh = repair shop. Ta'mirkār = repairman.
Ghabul kard = he accepted. Ghabule? = deal? Ghabul shodam = I passed (the exam accepted me).
Ghāyem sho! = hide! Ghāyem bāshak = hide-and-seek. Ghāyem kardan = to hide something.
Montazeram = I am waiting. Montazer-e chi hasti? = what are you waiting for? Montazeret mimunam = I will wait for you.
Boro esterāhat kon = go get some rest. Esterāhat-e motlagh = complete bed rest (doctor's orders).
Bidār sho! = wake up! Sobh-e zud bidār misham = I wake up early. Bidār = awake: bidāri? = are you up?
Moshkeli nist = no problem. Moshkel chiye? = what is the problem? Moshkel-e man in-e ke ... = my problem is that ...
Literally: way of solving. Rāh-e hall peydā kardim = we found a solution. Har moshkeli rāh-e hall dāre = every problem has one.
Forsat nadāram = I have not had the chance. Az forsat estefāde kon = seize the opportunity.
From French chance. Shāns āvordam = I got lucky. Khosh-shāns = lucky, bad-shāns = unlucky. Shānsi = at random.
Ettefāgh oftād = it happened (events fall in Persian). Che ettefāghi oftāde? = what happened? Ettefāghi = by chance.
Be che dalil? = for what reason? Bi-dalil = for no reason. Dalilesh ine ke ... = the reason is that ...
Natije chi shod? = how did it turn out? Dar natije = as a result. Natije-ye emtehān = exam results.
Hadafet chiye? = what is your goal? Bi-hadaf = aimless. Hadaf-gozāri = goal-setting.
Barnāmat chiye? = what is your plan? (for tonight, for life). Barnāme-rizi = planning. TV barnāme = program.
Ide-ye khubiye = good idea. Ide dāri? = got any ideas? The everyday word is fekr.
Che jur? = what kind, how? Ye jurāyi = somehow, sort of. Jur dar nemiyād = it does not add up. Hame jur = all sorts.
Che now'i? = what type? Az in now' = of this kind. Tanavvo' = variety, its cousin.
Shekl-e man = looks like me (my shape). In shekli = like this, this way. Be har shekl = in any case.
Andāzash chande? = what size is it? Be andāze-ye kāfi = enough (to the sufficient measure). Andāze gereftan = to measure.
Ye meghdār = some amount, a bit. Che meghdār? = how much? Meghdār-e ziād = a large amount.
Ghesmat-e avval = part one. But also fate: ghesmat nabud = it was not meant to be, the phrase that closes every missed chance.
Spoken: tike. Ye tike nun = a piece of bread. Tike tike = in pieces. Tike andākhtan = to make a jab, throw shade.
Goruh-e musighi = music band. Kār-e goruhi = teamwork. Goruh-e telegrāmi = the group chat.
Ye daste gol = a bouquet (bunch of flowers). Daste-ye dar = door handle. Daste-jam'i = all together.
Be ye shart = on one condition. Shart mibandam = I bet. Sharti ke gozāshti = the condition you set.
Gharār dāram = I have an appointment (or a date). Gharār-e sā'at-e panj = the five-o'clock meeting. Gharār-e = it is supposed to (gharāre biād = he is supposed to come).
Dastur-e pokht = recipe (cooking instructions). Dastur-e zabān = grammar. Be dastur-e ki? = on whose orders?
Hagh bā to-e = you are right (the right is with you). Haghet-e = you deserve it (either way). Hagh-e man = my right.
Vazife-ye man-e = it is my duty (also the modest reply to thanks: anjām-e vazife bud). Vazife-shenās = dutiful.
Eshtebāh kardam = I made a mistake. Eshtebāh gereftid = you have the wrong number. Eshtebāhi = by mistake.
Gonāh-e man chiye? = what is my fault? Gonāh dāre = have pity on him (wronging him would be a sin). Bi-gonāh = innocent.
The magic phrase: eyb nadāre = no problem, it is fine, do not worry about it. Bi-eyb = flawless.
Bahāne nayār = no excuses. Bahāne-gir = fussy, always finding pretexts (said of toddlers and bosses).
Shukhi kardam = just kidding. Shukhi nakon! = you are joking! Jeddi migam, shukhi nist = I am serious, no joke.
Jeddi? = seriously? Jeddi migi? = are you for real? Jeddi bāsh = be serious. Opposite of shukhi.
Mas'ale in-e ke ... = the issue is that ... Mas'ale-i nist = no big deal. Also the schoolbook math problem.
Mowzu' chiye? = what is this about? Avaz kardan-e mowzu' = changing the subject. Mowzu'-e jeddi = a serious matter.
Dar-e asli = the main door. Ghazā-ye asli = the main course. Noskhe-ye asli = the original copy. From asl, root/origin.
Fargh dāre = it is different (it has difference). Che farghi mikone? = what difference does it make? Fargh-e in do = the difference between these two.
Shabih-e man-e = he looks like me. Shabih-e ham = alike. Shabāhat = resemblance.
Masalan (for example) is its adverb. Ye mesāl bezan = give an example. Bi-mesāl = without equal.
Tajrobe dāram = I have experience. Bā-tajrobe = experienced. Tajrobe kardan = to experience.
Khātere-hā-ye khub = good memories. Khātere sākhtim = we made memories. Be khāter-e (because of) shares the root.
The word on screen when the movie ends: pāyān. Pāyān-e hafte = week's end. Bi-pāyān = endless.
Nokte-ye mohem = the key point. Nokte-sanj = sharp-witted, catcher of subtleties. Ye nokte begam? = may I make a point?
Dowlat-e irān = the Iranian government. Dowlati = state-run, public. Originally: fortune, wealth.
Khalāf-e ghānun = against the law. Ghānuni = legal. From Greek kanon, cousin of English canon.
Jang-e jahāni = world war. Jang-e irān o arāgh: the 1980s war that shaped modern Iran. Jangidan = to fight.
Solh kardan = to make peace. Jang o solh = War and Peace. Solh-āmiz = peaceful.
Artesh-e irān = the Iranian army. Arteshi = military man.
Enghelāb-e 57: the 1979 revolution (year 1357). Meydān-e enghelāb = Revolution Square, Tehran's book-market hub.
Borj-e āzādi = the Azadi Tower, Tehran's white marble gate. Āzād (free) + -i makes the noun.
Mellat-e irān = the Iranian nation. Melli = national: kārt-e melli, tim-e melli (the national team).
Jāme'e-shenāsi = sociology (society-knowing). Jāme'e-ye irān = Iranian society.
Farhang-e irāni = Persian culture. A farhang is also a dictionary: farhang-e loghat. Bā-farhang = cultured.
Tārikh-e irān = the history of Iran. Tārikh-e emruz chande? = what is today's date? Tārikhi = historic.
Din-dār = religious (religion-holding). Bi-din = irreligious. Din o donyā = faith and the world.
Namāz khāndan = to pray (prayer is read). Namāz-e jom'e = Friday prayers. The call to it: azān.
Eyd-e shomā mobārak = happy holiday (the Nowruz greeting). Eydi = the holiday cash gift elders slip into your hand.
Māh-e ramazān = the fasting month. Ruze gereftan = to fast. Eftār = the sunset fast-breaking meal, a nightly feast.
Behesht zir-e pā-ye mādarān ast = paradise lies beneath mothers' feet. English paradise is itself old Persian: paradaiza, a walled garden.
Boro be jahannam = go to hell. Injā jahannam-e = this place is hell (Tehran in August). Jahannami = infernal.
Spoken: sheytun. To kids, fondly: ey sheytun! = you little devil! Sheytuni nakon = no mischief.
Bā-adab = polite, bi-adab = rude. Adabiāt = literature; in Persian, good manners and letters share one root.
Ehterām gozāshtan = to pay respect. Bā ehterām = respectfully (letter-closing). Ehterām-e bozorgtarhā = respect for elders, rule one.
Behesh e'temād dāram = I trust him. E'temād be nafs = self-confidence. Ghābel-e e'temād = trustworthy.
Āb (water) + ru (face): the water of the face. Āberum raft = I lost face. Āberu-dāri = keeping up appearances, a serious social art.
Ruz (day) + nāme (letter): the day-letter. Ruznāme-negār = journalist.
Dowre-ye ghājār = the Qajar era. Dowre-ye zabān = a language course. Un dowre = back in that period.
Spoken: sākhtemun. From sākhtan, to build. Sākhtemun-e boland = a tall building.
Mantaghe-ye māst = it is our turf. Mantaghe-ye āzād = free zone. In mantaghe ro balad nistam = I do not know this area.
Bachche-mahal = neighborhood kid, someone from your block. Mahalle-ye ghadimi = the old quarter.
Atrāf-e shahr = around the city. In atrāf = around here. Atrāfiān = one's circle, the people around someone.
Vāghe'an? = really? Vāghe'an mamnun = truly, thank you. From vāghe', reality: dar vāghe' = in fact.
Albatte! = of course! Albatte ke miām = of course I will come. Softens or confirms, endlessly useful.
The explain-everything word: ya'ni chi? = what does that mean? Ya'ni miād? = so, will he come? In ya'ni jang = this means war.
Masalan chi? = like what? From mesāl, example. Also sarcastic: masalan dāri dars mikhuni = you are supposedly studying.
With negatives: aslan nemidunam = I have no idea at all. Aslan velesh kon = you know what, forget it entirely.
Hatman biā = definitely come. Hatman khaste-i = you must be tired. The commitment word of Iranian plans.
Hattā man = even me. Hattā ye kalame nagoft = he did not say even one word.
Daghighan! = exactly! Daghighan hamin-e = that is exactly it. From daghigh, precise (cousin of daghighe, minute).
Taghriban tamum-e = it is almost done. Taghriban sad nafar = roughly a hundred people.
Ma'mulan sobh-hā varzesh mikonam = I usually exercise mornings. From ma'muli, ordinary: ādam-e ma'muli = a regular person.
Gāhi vaghtā = sometimes, now and then (spoken). Gāh-be-gāh = from time to time. From gāh, time/moment.
Zāheran rafte = apparently he has left. From zāher, appearance: zāheresh khube = it looks fine (on the surface).
Mota'assefāne na = unfortunately not. Its cousin: mota'assefam = I am sorry (sympathy, not apology).
Khoshbakhtāne hame khuban = fortunately everyone is fine. Khosh + bakht (luck) + -āne, mirror of mota'assefāne.
The formal connector of essays and news: banābarin tasmim gereftim ... = therefore we decided ... Speech just says pas.

fields.back: because of, for the sake of
fields.extra: Be khāter-e to = for your sake, because of you. Be khāter-e hamin = that is why. Bekhāter-e chi? = on account of what?
Darbāre-ye chi? = about what? Darbāre-ye irān = about Iran. Spoken twin: rāje' be (rāje' be to harf mizadim = we were talking about you).
Bedun-e shak = without a doubt. Bedun-e to = without you (the title of half of all Persian pop songs).
Be jā-ye man boro = go instead of me. Be jā-ye inke ghor bezani, komak kon = instead of grumbling, help.

fields.back: on the contrary; upside down, reversed
fields.extra: Bar'aks-e to = the opposite of you. Aksesh bar'aks-e = the picture is upside down. Bar (over) + aks (image).
Mesl-e man = like me. Mesl-e inke = it seems that (like that ...). Mesl-e hamishe = as always. Top-100 word; glue everywhere.
Engār khābam = it is as if I am dreaming. Engār na engār = as if nothing happened, total indifference (a perfect idiom).
Belākhare umadi! = you finally came! Belākhare chi shod? = so what happened in the end?
Kholāse ke ... = long story short ... Kholāse-ye dāstān = the plot summary. Kholāse konam = let me wrap up.
Spoken: mage. Mage nagoftam? = did I not tell you? Mage mishe? = how is that even possible? Magar inke = unless.
Opens written questions: āyā midānestid ...? = did you know ...? Speech drops it and just raises the voice.
Momkene = it is possible, may be: momkene biād = he might come. Gheyr-e momken = impossible. Momkene lotfan ...? = could you please ...?
The Persian OK: bāshe, miām = fine, I will come. Literally: may it be (from budan). Bāshe bābā = fine, fine!
Now (new) + ruz (day): the new day. Begins at the spring equinox, exact to the minute; 3000 years old and counting. Two weeks of visits, gifts, and the haft-sin table.
Shab-e yaldā: the year's longest night. Families stay up with pomegranates, watermelon, and the poems of Hafez until the sun wins.

fields.back: please, go ahead; here you are
fields.extra: The universal courtesy verb: offering a seat, food, entry, or the right of way. Befarmāid tu = please come in. Answering the phone: befarmāid?
